alt_forms.note: Loanword from English 'turn'; common in video games and modern board games. Less historically rooted than 手番.

definitions.gloss_en: one's turn to act; the right and responsibility to move
definitions.gloss_jp: {自|じ}{分|ぶん}が{行|こう}{動|どう}できる{順|じゅん}{番|ばん}
definitions.explanation_en: The period in which one player exercises agency while others wait. Originating in shogi and go, where it marks whose move is next.
definitions.implicit_notes: 手番 frames the turn as obligation as well as right — it is whose task it is to move, not merely whose opportunity.
definitions.orthography_notes: Native Japanese compound: 手 (hand / move) + 番 (turn / one's place in sequence). The shogi/go origin predates all modern miniature wargaming. ターン Wikipedia Japan (ターン (ゲーム)) explicitly traces the origin of the 手番 concept to shogi.
definitions.game_setting_notes: In shogi and go, 手番 refers specifically to the right to move next. In board and miniature wargames, the meaning extends to the full scope of a player's action phase within a round.

usage_jp: {手|て}{番|ばん}プレイヤー (active player); {手|て}{番|ばん}を{交|か}わす (pass the turn); あなたの{手|て}{番|ばん}です (it is your turn)
usage_en: "turn" as a noun; sometimes "your move" in contexts derived from abstract strategy games.
comparative_notes: ターン (taan) is the dominant loanword in video games and many modern board games. 手番 is preferred in contexts emphasizing the Japanese game-design lineage or for explicit shogi/go cross-reference. Both are IP-safe.

examples.japanese: {手|て}{番|ばん}プレイヤーは{行|こう}{動|どう}を{実|じっ}{行|こう}する。
examples.english: The active player executes an action.

cross_references.note: 手番プレイヤー — the active player
非手番プレイヤー — the reactive player
フェイズ — subdivision of a turn into phases
